summary: This short post addresses two unrelated topics. First, Joel responds to a question about why Excel requires so much disk space, explaining that the bloat comes from shared Office components and bundled applications like Internet Explorer, not from Excel itself. He dismisses the concern, noting that Office takes up only 1% of his disk space. Second, he shares an amusing observation about deceptive advertising, referencing a Simpsons writer's concept of ads where the 'word-to-falsehood ratio approaches one,' and teases a piece of junk mail from Earthlink.
key_points: Excel's large install size comes primarily from shared Office components and bundled applications like IE, not from Excel's own code (the EXE is under 7 MB)
Software bloat concerns are often overblown relative to available disk space — 190MB was only 1% of his disk at the time
Deceptive marketing can be so concentrated that nearly every word in an ad contains a falsehood
money_quotes: The actual Excel EXE itself is under 7 MB.
But who cares? It's a great app and it's 1% of my disk space.
George Meyer, one of the writers on the Simpsons, likes to collect examples of advertising in which 'the word-to-falsehood ratio approaches one.'
It's not from the country, there is no crock. Two words, two lies.
key_insight: Software bloat is often misattributed to the core application when the real culprit is shared components and bundled dependencies, and the practical impact on users is typically negligible.
